title: Canhıraş
subtitle: An autonomous fixed-wing UAV I built alone, while technically having a team.
team: Team Bangu · funded by Eurasia University
specs: 2× MG996 + 3× EMAX ES08MA II metal-gear servos

gallery.caption: Full CAD assembly — the twin-tractor layout that defined the airframe.
Side profile: high wing, single vertical stabiliser, tricycle gear.
Nose detail — Pi HQ camera under a carbon prop and a 3D-printed motor mount.
The full electrical schematic I drew — air segment on top, ground station below.
The very first sketch: weights, servo counts and incidence angles worked out by hand.

body: Canhıraş is a twin-engine fixed-wing UAV I designed, wired and built from the ground up for Teknofest. The name is Turkish for something along the lines of "bloodcurdling", which felt appropriately dramatic for what was, functionally, a very expensive flying laptop with wings. It flew under Team Bangu ⚡, funded by Eurasia University.

Now, the elephant in the hangar: I had a team. On paper. In practice, the team was there to learn, and I was there to do literally everything — the CAD, the wiring, the code, the config, the mild sobbing at 3 a.m. So when I say "we" built a drone, what I mean is that I built a drone while several lovely people watched supportively... Well they were useful because you needed a "Team" to join Teknofest so. Best systems-engineering bootcamp of my life, would recommend, would not do again.
The airframe (a.k.a. teaching a laptop to fly)

I modelled the whole aircraft in SolidWorks: high wing, two wing-mounted tractor motors, a single tail fin, and tricycle landing gear so it could take off from a runway like a dignified adult instead of being yeeted off a catapult. Two engines wasn't showing off — it's redundancy, plus it kept the nose free for a camera. The guts weighed about 3.5 kg before the airframe, so most of "design" was me negotiating with gravity over individual grams and trying to stop the centre of gravity from wandering off because I'd bolted a whole Nvidia computer. Gravity won some rounds.

Flight brains: a Cube Orange with a Here2 GNSS puck handling stabilisation, waypoints and the all-important "please don't crash" failsafes. Bolted next to it — the part I'm genuinely proud of — an NVIDIA Jetson TX2 as a companion computer, so the aircraft could do computer vision onboard instead of politely mailing every frame back to the ground and waiting. A Raspberry Pi HQ camera feeds it. A 6S 13,000 mAh LiPo the size of a small brick powers the whole circus through a distribution board, an arming switch, and a buck regulator that calmly turns 22 volts into a civilised 5. Two SunnySky brushless motors on Skywalker 80A ESCs pull; a fleet of MG996 and EMAX servos wiggle the control surfaces.

And that is right, I drew every line of it by hand. It splits into an air half and a ground half, and drawing it was the only reason I understood my own aircraft. You cannot wire twenty components on vibes alone. Well I tried but it goes poorly.
Eyes and radio (three of them, because trust issues)

The radio setup was its own small saga. A Ubiquiti Rocket 5 GHz bridge for fat video and vision data, an RFD900x for long-range telemetry that refuses to die, and a RadioLink receiver for good old-fashioned "grab the sticks" RC override. Three independent radios, because losing one link and therefore an entire aircraft is the kind of lesson you only want to learn in theory. On the ground I built the matching half: a laptop, an 8-port gigabit switch, the paired dish, an RFD900x and an AT10 transmitter — an entire tiny office network whose sole purpose in life was talking to one drone in a field.

Canhıraş is my go-to answer for "can you own a whole system end to end?" Aerodynamics, mechanical, electrical, embedded, networking, a dash of computer vision — all of it had to get along, and when it didn't, the solution was obviously, almost always staring at the Black Sea of Trabzon blankly until i figured it out. It also taught me that carrying a team solo is a real cost and not a personality trait to be proud of. But the aircraft was mine, nose to tail...
